0: <title>Life cycle assessment of manure management and nutrient recycling from a Chinese pig farm </title>
Waste Management & Research 2014, Vol 32(1) 4-12 © The Author(s) 2013 Reprints and permissions: sagepub.co.uk/journalsPermissions.nav DOI: 10.1177/0734242X13512715 wmr.sagepub.com
Yiming Luo Heinz Stichnothe³, Frank Schuchardt³, Guoxue Li1, Roxana Mendoza Huaitalla4 and Wen Xu1
1: Driven by the growing numbers of intensified pig farms around cities in China, there are problems of nutrient surplus and shortage of arable land for utilising the manure. Hence, sustainable livestock systems with effective manure management are needed. The objective of this study is to compare the existing manure treatment of a typical pig farm in Beijing area (separate collection of faeces; 'Gan qing fen' system) with an alternative system and to identify the nutrients flow of the whole farm in order to quantify environmental burdens and to estimate the arable land required for sustainable nutrients recycling. Life cycle assessment is used for this purpose. Acidification potential (AP), eutrophication potential (EP) and global warming potential (GWP) are analysed in detail; the functional unit is the annual production of the pig farm. The results show that the cropland area demand for sustainable land application of the effluent can be reduced from 238 to 139 ha with the alternative system. It is possible to transfer 29% of total nitrogen, 87% of phosphorus, 34% of potassium and 75% of magnesium to the compost, and to reduce the total AP, EP and GWP of manure management on the farm by 64.1%, 96.7% and 22%, respectively, compared with the current system. Besides an effective manure management system, a full inventory of the regional nutrients flow is needed for sustainable development of livestock systems around big cities in China.
2: Pig farm, life cycle assessment, nutrients, manure management, effluent
3: <header>Introduction </header>

Owing to rapid urbanisation in China, there is a growing number of intensified pig farms around cities. One typical characteristic of these farms is the lack of arable land for utilising the manure. Uncontrolled discharge of surplus nutrients causes considerable environment impacts (Aarnink and Verstegen, 2007; Nguyen et al., 2012; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). Li et al. (2009) investi- gated the capacity of arable land for manure application, and esti- mated that the maximum capacity of animal production in Shunyi district of Beijing should be 302,000 heads of pigs, just one-third of the actual production. Sustainable livestock systems with effective manure management are needed.

The long-term storage of manure is just as poorly economi- cally feasible as its transport over long distances. Hence, the sur- plus nutrients must be converted into a stable and transportable form. Composting enables the export of nutrients from areas with high livestock density to arable land. Another option is the cas- cade application-particularly of liquid manure-by disposing it in an anaerobic digester followed by composting of the digester sludge in a composting plant. The 'Gan qing fen' (cleaning the manure dryly) system is widely used in dry areas in north China to reduce water consumption. In this system, the solid phase is separated and consists of faeces and some urine, which is suitable for composting (Schuchardt et al., 2011). However, anaerobic digestion is also widely used for pig manure or pig farm effluent

treatment in China. But, the effluents from biogas plants with high nutrient contents are frequently discharged to the local envi- ronment owing to the shortage of arable land nearby, which, con- sequently, causes significant environmental burdens. The need for proper manure management of pig farms has been raised (Bayo et al., 2012; Nguyen et al., 2012).

Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been used for environmental assessment of pig farms on different aspects. Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) compared three different farming methods of pig production using the LCA method; eutrophication and acidification were considered as the two main impacts of concern for pig production. According to Prapaspongsa et al. (2010), the

main impact categories arising from pig manure management were global warming, aquatic eutrophication, respiratory inor- ganics and terrestrial eutrophication. Ammonia (NH3) and meth- ane (CH4) emissions from pig slurry processing or storage are the important parameters that affect environmental performance in terms of eutrophication, acidification and climate change (De Vries et al., 2012a; Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009). LCA has been used to investigate different pig manure processing technologies, such as anaerobic digestion of pig manure with different kinds of crops or wastes (De Vries et al., 2012b) and to compare anaerobic digestion, incineration and thermal gasification of pig manure (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). However, limited research has sur- veyed nutrient flow and integrated manure management systems in pig farms in China on a life cycle basis.

Nutrient recycling in pig farms, appropriate pig manure man- agement and alternative technologies adapted to local conditions are of crucial importance when environmental impacts are to be reduced. The focus of our investigation is to identify an appropri- ate option of manure management for pig farms in Beijing area with respect to recycling nutrients and reducing environmental burdens efficiently.

10: <header>Methods </header>
The pig production system and manure management

Current manure management (Figure 1). To collect the pigs excreta, the so-called 'Gan qing fen' technique is used in the sta- bles (Schuchardt et al., 2011), a system mandatory in the area of Beijing. The faeces are collected manually daily from the con- crete floor and transported to a biogas plant during 9 months of the year (from September to May). During summer (June- August) the faeces are treated in a composting plant with the addition of sawdust as a bulking agent. The urine and a portion of

the faeces are flushed with water to a biogas plant for the whole year to produce biogas for cooking in a nearby village. A certain amount of poultry manure from another farm is used as a supple- ment to the biogas plant to enhance the biogas yield during the cold season. There are three anaerobic fermentation tanks work- ing year-round, with a total volume of 2200 m³ in the biogas plant. The average fermentation temperature in the tanks is about 35°C. The effluent of the biogas plant is treated in aeration and sedimentation ponds from March to November before being col- lected in an open lagoon; then, the effluent from the lagoon dis- charges to the surface water. During winter the aeration process is interrupted and the liquid is discharged into the lagoon directly. Sludge from the biogas plant is used in the composting plant together with pig manure and sawdust. The compost is used as organic fertiliser on cropland of the farm.

Alternative manure management system (Figure2). The alter- native system aims to enhance the amount of nutrients in the compost, use the effluent as fertiliser and reduce the environmen- tal pollution. The main changes of the alternative system are that all of the pig faeces are used throughout the year in the compost- ing plant; the liquid from pig stables is used in the biogas plant without any addition of faeces or poultry manure; the effluent from the biogas plant is stored in covered ponds to avoid CH4 and NH3 emissions; and is used finally for land application. The bio- gas is exported to the village nearby as described above. Chopped corn stalks as a bulking agent and superphosphate as nitrogen conservation additives are used in the composting plant instead of sawdust. Compost can be sold and thus offers the possibility of exporting surplus nutrients to other farms with nutrient demand.

Current manure management with effluent for land applica- tion. If enough croplands are available, the effluent from the bio- gas plant can be utilised for irrigation, which would reduce the fertiliser demand, as well as emissions to the aquatic environ- ment. In this scenario, the flow sheet in the pig farm is the same

as the current system (Figure 1), but the effluent from the lagoon is assumed to be used for land application corresponding to the alternative manure management system. For the calculation of the area demand, the same crop rotation is assumed as that for the alternative manure management system.
For the LCA, the pig production system is subdivided into three modules:

<list>1. Pig production, encompasses the feed supply and the pig sta- bles (aeration, heating, emissions) 
2. Biogas plant including waste water treatment 
3. Composting plant.</list>

A certain amount of poultry manure from another farm is used as a supple- ment to the biogas plant to enhance the biogas yield during the cold season. There are three anaerobic fermentation tanks work- ing year-round, with a total volume of 2200 m³ in the biogas plant. The average fermentation temperature in the tanks is about 35°C. The effluent of the biogas plant is treated in aeration and sedimentation ponds from March to November before being col- lected in an open lagoon; then, the effluent from the lagoon dis- charges to the surface water. During winter the aeration process is interrupted and the liquid is discharged into the lagoon directly. Sludge from the biogas plant is used in the composting plant together with pig manure and sawdust. The compost is used as organic fertiliser on cropland of the farm.

Alternative manure management system (Figure2). The alter- native system aims to enhance the amount of nutrients in the compost, use the effluent as fertiliser and reduce the environmen- tal pollution. The main changes of the alternative system are that all of the pig faeces are used throughout the year in the compost- ing plant; the liquid from pig stables is used in the biogas plant without any addition of faeces or poultry manure; the effluent from the biogas plant is stored in covered ponds to avoid CH4 and NH3 emissions; and is used finally for land application. The bio- gas is exported to the village nearby as described above. Chopped corn stalks as a bulking agent and superphosphate as nitrogen conservation additives are used in the composting plant instead of sawdust. Compost can be sold and thus offers the possibility of exporting surplus nutrients to other farms with nutrient demand.

Current manure management with effluent for land applica- tion. If enough croplands are available, the effluent from the bio- gas plant can be utilised for irrigation, which would reduce the fertiliser demand, as well as emissions to the aquatic environ- ment. In this scenario, the flow sheet in the pig farm is the same

as the current system (Figure 1), but the effluent from the lagoon is assumed to be used for land application corresponding to the alternative manure management system. For the calculation of the area demand, the same crop rotation is assumed as that for the alternative manure management system.
For the LCA, the pig production system is subdivided into three modules:

<list>1. Pig production, encompasses the feed supply and the pig sta- bles (aeration, heating, emissions) 
2. Biogas plant including waste water treatment 
3. Composting plant.</list>
13: <header>Methods </header>
The pig production system and manure management

Current manure management (Figure 1). To collect the pigs excreta, the so-called 'Gan qing fen' technique is used in the sta- bles (Schuchardt et al., 2011), a system mandatory in the area of Beijing. The faeces are collected manually daily from the con- crete floor and transported to a biogas plant during 9 months of the year (from September to May). During summer (June- August) the faeces are treated in a composting plant with the addition of sawdust as a bulking agent. The urine and a portion of

the faeces are flushed with water to a biogas plant for the whole year to produce biogas for cooking in a nearby village. A certain amount of poultry manure from another farm is used as a supple- ment to the biogas plant to enhance the biogas yield during the cold season. There are three anaerobic fermentation tanks work- ing year-round, with a total volume of 2200 m³ in the biogas plant. The average fermentation temperature in the tanks is about 35°C. The effluent of the biogas plant is treated in aeration and sedimentation ponds from March to November before being col- lected in an open lagoon; then, the effluent from the lagoon dis- charges to the surface water. During winter the aeration process is interrupted and the liquid is discharged into the lagoon directly. Sludge from the biogas plant is used in the composting plant together with pig manure and sawdust. The compost is used as organic fertiliser on cropland of the farm.

Alternative manure management system (Figure2). The alter- native system aims to enhance the amount of nutrients in the compost, use the effluent as fertiliser and reduce the environmen- tal pollution. The main changes of the alternative system are that all of the pig faeces are used throughout the year in the compost- ing plant; the liquid from pig stables is used in the biogas plant without any addition of faeces or poultry manure; the effluent from the biogas plant is stored in covered ponds to avoid CH4 and NH3 emissions; and is used finally for land application. The bio- gas is exported to the village nearby as described above. Chopped corn stalks as a bulking agent and superphosphate as nitrogen conservation additives are used in the composting plant instead of sawdust. Compost can be sold and thus offers the possibility of exporting surplus nutrients to other farms with nutrient demand.

Current manure management with effluent for land applica- tion. If enough croplands are available, the effluent from the bio- gas plant can be utilised for irrigation, which would reduce the fertiliser demand, as well as emissions to the aquatic environ- ment. In this scenario, the flow sheet in the pig farm is the same

as the current system (Figure 1), but the effluent from the lagoon is assumed to be used for land application corresponding to the alternative manure management system. For the calculation of the area demand, the same crop rotation is assumed as that for the alternative manure management system.
For the LCA, the pig production system is subdivided into three modules:

<list>1. Pig production, encompasses the feed supply and the pig sta- bles (aeration, heating, emissions) 
2. Biogas plant including waste water treatment 
3. Composting plant.</list>
14: LCA is a valuable tool for the environmental evaluation of agri- cultural systems (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Brentrup et al., 2000; Kloverpris et al., 2008; Stichnothe and Schuchardt, 2011). This study uses LCA following the ISO standards 14040:2006 and 14044:2006.

The LCA modelling was carried out in GaBi 4.4 (PE-International, Leinfelden-Echterdingen, Germany); using the CML 2 (2007) baseline method for estimating the environ- mental impacts. The CML 2 (2007) method uses updated Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change characterisation factors for calculating the global warming potential (GWP). The environmental impact categories investigated in detail are GWP, eutrophication potential (EP) and acidification potential (AP).
15: The goal of the study is to evaluate environmental impacts of two manure management systems for pig production in China. The selected farm is a large-sized pig farms located in the sub- urban Shunyi district in Beijing, China, which has no, or lim- ited, cropland areas for sustainable manure utilisation. The primary objective of this study is to compare the existing manure treatment with an alternative system within the scope of the whole farm.
16: The system boundary is from cradle-to-gate and the functional unit is the annual production of a typical pig farm in Beijing area. The farm has a stock of 1037 livestock units (LU) (1 500 kg life weight) and produces 1956 LU annually.
17: The pig farm is a multi-output system; the outputs are pigs and biogas, as well as compost. Biogas is currently sold as energy carrier and compost is traded as organic fertiliser and soil improver. ISO 14040/44 allows physical allocation, for example based on mass or energy content and economic allocation, but recommends avoiding allocation. System expansion is a common concept in LCA and is mentioned as the preferred option for

multi-output systems in order to avoid allocation according to ISO14040/44. The system expansion approach is described in detail in the Life Cycle Data System (ILCD) Handbook (European Commission Joint Research Centre, 2010). In this study, system expansion is used and it is assumed that CH4 replaces natural gas and nutrients in compost, and effluents replace mineral fertiliser.
18: <header>Life cycle inventory and source of data </header>

Among the four stages of an LCA, the second step consists of an inventory of input/output data: resources used and substances emitted throughout the entire life cycle of the product are studied. The relevance of an LCA is strongly linked with the quality of the inventory analysis (Basset-Mens et al., 2006; Weidema and Wesnaes, 1996).

Figures 1 and 2 show the life cycle diagrams of the pig farm with the current and alternative manure systems respectively, encompassing input materials like feed, ancillary materials and energy, including transport to the farm, as well as the management of manure, which was anaerobic digestion and composting.

Pig production. Mass and volume flows (pigs, feed, water, waste water, faeces), as well as energy consumption data, were collected from a pig farm manager in 2011. The composition and nutrients content in feed, waste water and pig faeces were directly measured. Mean values of the analysed samples are shown in Table 1. NH3 emissions from stables were aggregated values for the whole year based on average emission rate (178 mg h-¹ per pig head) measured by passive samplers in summer and winter, according to the method reported by Philippe et al. (2007). Inven- tory data of the pig production are shown in Table 2. The premix composition used in the feed was unknown. Therefore, a correc- tion factor [mass total feed/(mass total feed - mass premix)] was introduced for the remaining feed compounds in order to account for the omitted premix. Output data of antibiotics and vaccine were not available; therefore, the ecotoxicity and toxicity impacts were underestimated in this study.

Biogas plant (combined with aeration plant). The correspond- ing data of manure management are given in Tables 3 and 4. For the current system, flow quantities, energy consumption and bio- gas yield were provided by the farm manager. The compositions of the effluent from the biogas plant and the aeration basin were directly measured. Owing to the interruption of the aeration in winter for 3 months, weighted average data have been used for the calculation of emissions for 1 year of operation. The effluent volume in each month of a year was assumed to be the same. The different nitrogen content of the input and output of the aeration plant can be explained by ammonia emission. Fugitive CH4 emis- sion from the biogas plant was complemented by a literature value, which was assumed to be 3% of the total CH4 yield for all the options (Flesch et al., 2011).

For the alternative manure management system, the input data for the biogas plant were calculated based on the mass flows according to Figure 2 and shown in Table 4. The composition of liquid manure and the degradation rate in the fermentation tanks
Table 1. Composition of input materials, faeces and waste water from stables.

DM: dry matter; ODM: organic dry matter; N: nitrogen; P: phosphorus; K: potassium; Mg: magnesium; n.d.: no data; TS: total solids; COD: chemical oxygen demand. aPrasad (1980) and Zhang et al. (2002). blhnat (2000).

were assumed to be the same for both management systems. However, the different handling of faeces would reduce the bio- degradable content of the input of the biogas plant that caused lower biogas yield. For the existing biogas system and the alter- native option, the biogas potential was assumed to be 0.35 m³ CH4/kg chemical oxygen demand (COD) removal (Stichnothe and Schuchardt, 2010). According to the COD removal effi- ciency at current status, the CH4 yield for the alternative system was calculated as follows (equation (1)):
CH4yield (m2) =M,xEx 0.35m2/ kg (1)
where M is the COD input mass for the alternative system, kg, and E is the COD removal efficiency.
Furthermore, the COD output for the alternative system was calculated according to equation (2):
where M2 is the COD output mass for the alternative system, kg, and M and E are the same as in equation (1).

Composting plant. Data on annual flow quantities in the com- posting plant were collected from the pig farm manager. The energy demand of the composting plant was calculated based on data from the equipment provider (Backhus EcoEngineers). For the current system (Table 3), electricity was used for turn- ing the machine during a composting period of 42 days, and diesel fuel was used for mixing and transport by wheel loader. For the alternative system (Table 4), the composting period was assumed to be the same as in the existing system, but the higher material quantity caused an increased energy demand of the chopping machine (capacity average: 10 m³/h; rated power: 7.5 kW). The compositions of raw materials for composting were directly measured and complemented by literature data as shown in Table 1 (Ihnat 2000; Prasad, 1980; Zhang et al., 2002). Emissions from the composting process using a mixture of pig faeces and chopped corn stalks were measured in 1.2 m³ composting trials. Data from trials with superphosphate as additive to reduce NH3 losses were used (Jiang et al., 2011; Luo et al., 2012) for the alternative system. The considered emissions [CH4, NH3 and nitrous oxide (N20)] and total
Table 2. Life cycle inventory of the pig production module.

Input||Output|Output Feed||Pigs to market (LU)|1956 Maize (t)|2666|Dead pigs (LU)|49 Soybean cake (t)|885|Faeces (t)|2240 Fish meal (t)|14|Dry matter (t)|607 Wheat bran (t)|265|Nitrogen (t)|23.28 Soya bean oil (t)|4|Phosphorus (t)|13.19 Premix (t)|160|Potassium (t)|6.86 Dry matter (t)|3582|Magnesium (t)|6.01 Nitrogen (t)|102.87|Waste water (m³)|35,000 Phosphorus (t)|23.64|Total solids (t)|770 Potassium (t)|26.15|COD (t)|402.50 Magnesium (t)|6.98|Nitrogen (t)|46.96 Water (m³|35,000|Phosphorus (t)|2.95 Energy||Potassium (t)|22.05 Electricity (kWh)|760,000|Magnesium (t)|2.19 Soft coal (t)|300|Emissions| Vaccine (t)|0.952|NH3 (t)|11.38 ||NH-N (t)|9.37
LU: livestock units; COD: chemical oxygen demand; NH3: ammonia; NH3-N: ammonia nitrogen.

nitrogen loss during composting for the two different options are shown in Table 5. Volatile organic compounds from com- posting were not measured and therefore not considered in the calculation. The amount of compost product for the alternative system was calculated on the basis of the results obtained from composting trials (dry matter content in final compost was 70%; 49% of dry matter was degraded during composting process).

In addition, several assumptions were used within the anal- ysis: (1) nitrogen losses which had not been detected at each stage were assumed to be NH3 emissions; (2) for system expansion it was assumed that all nutrients in the effluent and phosphorus, potassium and magnesium in the compost equalled conventional fertiliser, while the long-term nitrogen efficiency in the compost was 50% (Springer, 2011); (3) the toxicity effect of antibiotics and vaccine remaining in the effluent was not considered; (4) capital goods were not taken into account.

21: <header>Life cycle inventory and source of data </header>

Among the four stages of an LCA, the second step consists of an inventory of input/output data: resources used and substances emitted throughout the entire life cycle of the product are studied. The relevance of an LCA is strongly linked with the quality of the inventory analysis (Basset-Mens et al., 2006; Weidema and Wesnaes, 1996).

Figures 1 and 2 show the life cycle diagrams of the pig farm with the current and alternative manure systems respectively, encompassing input materials like feed, ancillary materials and energy, including transport to the farm, as well as the management of manure, which was anaerobic digestion and composting.

Pig production. Mass and volume flows (pigs, feed, water, waste water, faeces), as well as energy consumption data, were collected from a pig farm manager in 2011. The composition and nutrients content in feed, waste water and pig faeces were directly measured. Mean values of the analysed samples are shown in Table 1. NH3 emissions from stables were aggregated values for the whole year based on average emission rate (178 mg h-¹ per pig head) measured by passive samplers in summer and winter, according to the method reported by Philippe et al. (2007). Inven- tory data of the pig production are shown in Table 2. The premix composition used in the feed was unknown. Therefore, a correc- tion factor [mass total feed/(mass total feed - mass premix)] was introduced for the remaining feed compounds in order to account for the omitted premix. Output data of antibiotics and vaccine were not available; therefore, the ecotoxicity and toxicity impacts were underestimated in this study.

Biogas plant (combined with aeration plant). The correspond- ing data of manure management are given in Tables 3 and 4. For the current system, flow quantities, energy consumption and bio- gas yield were provided by the farm manager. The compositions of the effluent from the biogas plant and the aeration basin were directly measured. Owing to the interruption of the aeration in winter for 3 months, weighted average data have been used for the calculation of emissions for 1 year of operation. The effluent volume in each month of a year was assumed to be the same. The different nitrogen content of the input and output of the aeration plant can be explained by ammonia emission. Fugitive CH4 emis- sion from the biogas plant was complemented by a literature value, which was assumed to be 3% of the total CH4 yield for all the options (Flesch et al., 2011).

For the alternative manure management system, the input data for the biogas plant were calculated based on the mass flows according to Figure 2 and shown in Table 4. The composition of liquid manure and the degradation rate in the fermentation tanks
Table 1. Composition of input materials, faeces and waste water from stables.

DM: dry matter; ODM: organic dry matter; N: nitrogen; P: phosphorus; K: potassium; Mg: magnesium; n.d.: no data; TS: total solids; COD: chemical oxygen demand. aPrasad (1980) and Zhang et al. (2002). blhnat (2000).

were assumed to be the same for both management systems. However, the different handling of faeces would reduce the bio- degradable content of the input of the biogas plant that caused lower biogas yield. For the existing biogas system and the alter- native option, the biogas potential was assumed to be 0.35 m³ CH4/kg chemical oxygen demand (COD) removal (Stichnothe and Schuchardt, 2010). According to the COD removal effi- ciency at current status, the CH4 yield for the alternative system was calculated as follows (equation (1)):
CH4yield (m2) =M,xEx 0.35m2/ kg (1)
where M is the COD input mass for the alternative system, kg, and E is the COD removal efficiency.
Furthermore, the COD output for the alternative system was calculated according to equation (2):
where M2 is the COD output mass for the alternative system, kg, and M and E are the same as in equation (1).

Composting plant. Data on annual flow quantities in the com- posting plant were collected from the pig farm manager. The energy demand of the composting plant was calculated based on data from the equipment provider (Backhus EcoEngineers). For the current system (Table 3), electricity was used for turn- ing the machine during a composting period of 42 days, and diesel fuel was used for mixing and transport by wheel loader. For the alternative system (Table 4), the composting period was assumed to be the same as in the existing system, but the higher material quantity caused an increased energy demand of the chopping machine (capacity average: 10 m³/h; rated power: 7.5 kW). The compositions of raw materials for composting were directly measured and complemented by literature data as shown in Table 1 (Ihnat 2000; Prasad, 1980; Zhang et al., 2002). Emissions from the composting process using a mixture of pig faeces and chopped corn stalks were measured in 1.2 m³ composting trials. Data from trials with superphosphate as additive to reduce NH3 losses were used (Jiang et al., 2011; Luo et al., 2012) for the alternative system. The considered emissions [CH4, NH3 and nitrous oxide (N20)] and total
Table 2. Life cycle inventory of the pig production module.

24: <header>Life cycle inventory and source of data </header>

Among the four stages of an LCA, the second step consists of an inventory of input/output data: resources used and substances emitted throughout the entire life cycle of the product are studied. The relevance of an LCA is strongly linked with the quality of the inventory analysis (Basset-Mens et al., 2006; Weidema and Wesnaes, 1996).

Figures 1 and 2 show the life cycle diagrams of the pig farm with the current and alternative manure systems respectively, encompassing input materials like feed, ancillary materials and energy, including transport to the farm, as well as the management of manure, which was anaerobic digestion and composting.

Pig production. Mass and volume flows (pigs, feed, water, waste water, faeces), as well as energy consumption data, were collected from a pig farm manager in 2011. The composition and nutrients content in feed, waste water and pig faeces were directly measured. Mean values of the analysed samples are shown in Table 1. NH3 emissions from stables were aggregated values for the whole year based on average emission rate (178 mg h-¹ per pig head) measured by passive samplers in summer and winter, according to the method reported by Philippe et al. (2007). Inven- tory data of the pig production are shown in Table 2. The premix composition used in the feed was unknown. Therefore, a correc- tion factor [mass total feed/(mass total feed - mass premix)] was introduced for the remaining feed compounds in order to account for the omitted premix. Output data of antibiotics and vaccine were not available; therefore, the ecotoxicity and toxicity impacts were underestimated in this study.

Biogas plant (combined with aeration plant). The correspond- ing data of manure management are given in Tables 3 and 4. For the current system, flow quantities, energy consumption and bio- gas yield were provided by the farm manager. The compositions of the effluent from the biogas plant and the aeration basin were directly measured. Owing to the interruption of the aeration in winter for 3 months, weighted average data have been used for the calculation of emissions for 1 year of operation. The effluent volume in each month of a year was assumed to be the same. The different nitrogen content of the input and output of the aeration plant can be explained by ammonia emission. Fugitive CH4 emis- sion from the biogas plant was complemented by a literature value, which was assumed to be 3% of the total CH4 yield for all the options (Flesch et al., 2011).

For the alternative manure management system, the input data for the biogas plant were calculated based on the mass flows according to Figure 2 and shown in Table 4. The composition of liquid manure and the degradation rate in the fermentation tanks
Table 1. Composition of input materials, faeces and waste water from stables.

DM: dry matter; ODM: organic dry matter; N: nitrogen; P: phosphorus; K: potassium; Mg: magnesium; n.d.: no data; TS: total solids; COD: chemical oxygen demand. aPrasad (1980) and Zhang et al. (2002). blhnat (2000).

were assumed to be the same for both management systems. However, the different handling of faeces would reduce the bio- degradable content of the input of the biogas plant that caused lower biogas yield. For the existing biogas system and the alter- native option, the biogas potential was assumed to be 0.35 m³ CH4/kg chemical oxygen demand (COD) removal (Stichnothe and Schuchardt, 2010). According to the COD removal effi- ciency at current status, the CH4 yield for the alternative system was calculated as follows (equation (1)):
CH4yield (m2) =M,xEx 0.35m2/ kg (1)
where M is the COD input mass for the alternative system, kg, and E is the COD removal efficiency.
Furthermore, the COD output for the alternative system was calculated according to equation (2):
where M2 is the COD output mass for the alternative system, kg, and M and E are the same as in equation (1).

Composting plant. Data on annual flow quantities in the com- posting plant were collected from the pig farm manager. The energy demand of the composting plant was calculated based on data from the equipment provider (Backhus EcoEngineers). For the current system (Table 3), electricity was used for turn- ing the machine during a composting period of 42 days, and diesel fuel was used for mixing and transport by wheel loader. For the alternative system (Table 4), the composting period was assumed to be the same as in the existing system, but the higher material quantity caused an increased energy demand of the chopping machine (capacity average: 10 m³/h; rated power: 7.5 kW). The compositions of raw materials for composting were directly measured and complemented by literature data as shown in Table 1 (Ihnat 2000; Prasad, 1980; Zhang et al., 2002). Emissions from the composting process using a mixture of pig faeces and chopped corn stalks were measured in 1.2 m³ composting trials. Data from trials with superphosphate as additive to reduce NH3 losses were used (Jiang et al., 2011; Luo et al., 2012) for the alternative system. The considered emissions [CH4, NH3 and nitrous oxide (N20)] and total
Table 2. Life cycle inventory of the pig production module.

LU: livestock units; COD: chemical oxygen demand; NH3: ammonia; NH3-N: ammonia nitrogen.

nitrogen loss during composting for the two different options are shown in Table 5. Volatile organic compounds from com- posting were not measured and therefore not considered in the calculation. The amount of compost product for the alternative system was calculated on the basis of the results obtained from composting trials (dry matter content in final compost was 70%; 49% of dry matter was degraded during composting process).

In addition, several assumptions were used within the anal- ysis: (1) nitrogen losses which had not been detected at each stage were assumed to be NH3 emissions; (2) for system expansion it was assumed that all nutrients in the effluent and phosphorus, potassium and magnesium in the compost equalled conventional fertiliser, while the long-term nitrogen efficiency in the compost was 50% (Springer, 2011); (3) the toxicity effect of antibiotics and vaccine remaining in the effluent was not considered; (4) capital goods were not taken into account.

27: <header>Life cycle inventory and source of data </header>

Among the four stages of an LCA, the second step consists of an inventory of input/output data: resources used and substances emitted throughout the entire life cycle of the product are studied. The relevance of an LCA is strongly linked with the quality of the inventory analysis (Basset-Mens et al., 2006; Weidema and Wesnaes, 1996).

Figures 1 and 2 show the life cycle diagrams of the pig farm with the current and alternative manure systems respectively, encompassing input materials like feed, ancillary materials and energy, including transport to the farm, as well as the management of manure, which was anaerobic digestion and composting.

Pig production. Mass and volume flows (pigs, feed, water, waste water, faeces), as well as energy consumption data, were collected from a pig farm manager in 2011. The composition and nutrients content in feed, waste water and pig faeces were directly measured. Mean values of the analysed samples are shown in Table 1. NH3 emissions from stables were aggregated values for the whole year based on average emission rate (178 mg h-¹ per pig head) measured by passive samplers in summer and winter, according to the method reported by Philippe et al. (2007). Inven- tory data of the pig production are shown in Table 2. The premix composition used in the feed was unknown. Therefore, a correc- tion factor [mass total feed/(mass total feed - mass premix)] was introduced for the remaining feed compounds in order to account for the omitted premix. Output data of antibiotics and vaccine were not available; therefore, the ecotoxicity and toxicity impacts were underestimated in this study.

Biogas plant (combined with aeration plant). The correspond- ing data of manure management are given in Tables 3 and 4. For the current system, flow quantities, energy consumption and bio- gas yield were provided by the farm manager. The compositions of the effluent from the biogas plant and the aeration basin were directly measured. Owing to the interruption of the aeration in winter for 3 months, weighted average data have been used for the calculation of emissions for 1 year of operation. The effluent volume in each month of a year was assumed to be the same. The different nitrogen content of the input and output of the aeration plant can be explained by ammonia emission. Fugitive CH4 emis- sion from the biogas plant was complemented by a literature value, which was assumed to be 3% of the total CH4 yield for all the options (Flesch et al., 2011).

For the alternative manure management system, the input data for the biogas plant were calculated based on the mass flows according to Figure 2 and shown in Table 4. The composition of liquid manure and the degradation rate in the fermentation tanks
Table 1. Composition of input materials, faeces and waste water from stables.

DM: dry matter; ODM: organic dry matter; N: nitrogen; P: phosphorus; K: potassium; Mg: magnesium; n.d.: no data; TS: total solids; COD: chemical oxygen demand. aPrasad (1980) and Zhang et al. (2002). blhnat (2000).

were assumed to be the same for both management systems. However, the different handling of faeces would reduce the bio- degradable content of the input of the biogas plant that caused lower biogas yield. For the existing biogas system and the alter- native option, the biogas potential was assumed to be 0.35 m³ CH4/kg chemical oxygen demand (COD) removal (Stichnothe and Schuchardt, 2010). According to the COD removal effi- ciency at current status, the CH4 yield for the alternative system was calculated as follows (equation (1)):
CH4yield (m2) =M,xEx 0.35m2/ kg (1)
where M is the COD input mass for the alternative system, kg, and E is the COD removal efficiency.
Furthermore, the COD output for the alternative system was calculated according to equation (2):
where M2 is the COD output mass for the alternative system, kg, and M and E are the same as in equation (1).

Composting plant. Data on annual flow quantities in the com- posting plant were collected from the pig farm manager. The energy demand of the composting plant was calculated based on data from the equipment provider (Backhus EcoEngineers). For the current system (Table 3), electricity was used for turn- ing the machine during a composting period of 42 days, and diesel fuel was used for mixing and transport by wheel loader. For the alternative system (Table 4), the composting period was assumed to be the same as in the existing system, but the higher material quantity caused an increased energy demand of the chopping machine (capacity average: 10 m³/h; rated power: 7.5 kW). The compositions of raw materials for composting were directly measured and complemented by literature data as shown in Table 1 (Ihnat 2000; Prasad, 1980; Zhang et al., 2002). Emissions from the composting process using a mixture of pig faeces and chopped corn stalks were measured in 1.2 m³ composting trials. Data from trials with superphosphate as additive to reduce NH3 losses were used (Jiang et al., 2011; Luo et al., 2012) for the alternative system. The considered emissions [CH4, NH3 and nitrous oxide (N20)] and total
Table 2. Life cycle inventory of the pig production module.

nitrogen loss during composting for the two different options are shown in Table 5. Volatile organic compounds from com- posting were not measured and therefore not considered in the calculation. The amount of compost product for the alternative system was calculated on the basis of the results obtained from composting trials (dry matter content in final compost was 70%; 49% of dry matter was degraded during composting process).

In addition, several assumptions were used within the anal- ysis: (1) nitrogen losses which had not been detected at each stage were assumed to be NH3 emissions; (2) for system expansion it was assumed that all nutrients in the effluent and phosphorus, potassium and magnesium in the compost equalled conventional fertiliser, while the long-term nitrogen efficiency in the compost was 50% (Springer, 2011); (3) the toxicity effect of antibiotics and vaccine remaining in the effluent was not considered; (4) capital goods were not taken into account.

'current + land application', was introduced. The distribution of nutrients to river, cropland and compost was different for the three described manure management systems (Table 6). The alternative manure management system enabled the transfer of 29% of total nitrogen to the compost compared to only 6% cur- rently. For the other nutrients, the rates were 87% (phosphorus), 34% (potassium) and 75% (magnesium).

The typical crop rotation in the district of the farm is winter wheat/summer maize with a nutrient demand per ha and year of 338 kg nitrogen, 56 kg phosphorus, 263 kg potassium, and 50 kg magnesium. The cropland demand for sustainable land applica- tion of the effluent for the current system would be 238 ha yr -1 (limited by phosphorus demand) compared with 139 ha yr-1 (lim- ited by nitrogen demand) for the alternative system. For the man- agement system 'current + land application' the same area demand as for the alternative system was used for the LCA calcu- lation. The calculation here was based on the nutrient demand; over-fertilisation in the past would lead to an even higher

cropland demand. Antibiotics and heavy metals in manure or manure-based effluent as an alternative organic fertiliser for land application could have impacts on the agricultural environment (Kwon et al., 2011; Tulayakul et al., 2011). However, the impacts of these chemicals were not considered for the calculation in this study owing to missing data.

Based on the cropland demand, the amount of effluent for land application per year would be 14.3 L m-² (14.3 mm) for the current system and 24.4 L m-2 (24.4 mm) for the alternative sys- tem. It would not be a problem for the cropland in the farm dis- trict in Beijing area with a mean annual precipitation around 600

5: <header>Life cycle impact assessment </header>

All environmental impacts for the three manure management options are shown in Table 7. For the management system 'cur- rent + land application;, effluent partly used for land application led to considerable reduction of abiotic resource depletion poten- tial, AP, EP and GWP compared to the current system. All envi- ronmental impacts decreased from the current system to the alternative system, for example AP from 79.4 to 61.9 ton SO2-Eq, EP from 91.7 to 34.1 ton PO4-Eq, and GWP from 5714 to 5611 ton CO2-Eq. The AP, EP and GWP as the most relevant impact factors of each component of the farm are discussed in detail below.
Pig production. Table 8 shows the environment impact assess- ment results of each component (pig production, biogas/waste water treatment, composting) of the pig farm to total AP, EP and

GWP for the current status, the current status + land application, and the alternative system. The mass flows in the pig stables and the impacts on the environment were the same for all described systems. The results indicated that the pig production contrib- uted most to AP and GWP, particularly in the alternative waste management system (84% of total AP and 93% of total GWP from pig production). In pig production, feed for pigs contrib- uted the most to GWP (61%) in this study. Besides feed (which is difficult for the farm to change), NH3 emission was a hotspot with high impact: 35% of total AP in pig production was due to the emissions from pig stables.

Biogas plant (combined with aeration plant). In the current system, the module biogas plant/waste water treatment plant con- tributed 64% to total EP compared to 41% if a part of the effluent was used for land application, and only 0.2% for the alternative system (Table 8). The significant improvement in EP was due to enhanced nitrogen and phosphorus recovery, as well as utilisation of effluent instead of discharging directly to the river. For AP, the reduction from the current to the alternative system was 22%; for GWP it was only 1%.
Composting plant. As shown in Table 8, the AP, EP and GWP from the composting process increased in the alternative scenario

as a result of the increased raw materials input, while all pig fae- ces would be used for composting. Considering the nutrient recy- cling from compost as credit, the environmental impacts per ton compost production would be reduced from the current to the alternative system. Waste air emissions during composting were the hotspots for AP, EP and GWP from the composting plant in this study.

Leaving pig production aside, the EP purely from the alter- native manure management system would be reduced by 91.8% compared with the 'current + land application' system, and 96.7% compared with the current system; the GWP would be reduced by 17.5% and 22%, respectively; and the AP would be reduced by 64.1% compared to the current system, but there would be no improvement from the 'current + land application system to the alternative system. The improvements between the 'current + land application' system and the alternative could be attributed to the new mass flow of all pig faeces for composting. The small disadvantage in AP due to the higher NH3 emission in composting plant would be outbalanced by the improvement of EP and GWP. It was possible to export part of the nutrients in compost from the pig farm with lower envi- ronmental impacts.

For the whole farm, there were improvements in total AP, EP and GWP by moving from the current manure management sys- tem to the alternative system. Nutrient recovery from effluents and faeces brought about a significant reduction in the environ- mental impacts of the farm. The alternative system was more environmental friendly in total. Feed was a hotspot for the main environmental impact categories of the farm, especially GWP, in this study. Similar results were reported by Eriksson et al. (2005) and Stone et al. (2011). However, there were other factors, such as manure mass flows, emissions and energy supply, that could be optimised by the farm to reduce the impact on the environ- ment. Similarly to the research by Prapaspongsa et al. (2010), the results of this study indicate that one of the effective ways for impact reduction in a pig farm is to enhance the nutrient and energy recovery at every stage, including in the control of NH3 and greenhouse gas emissions.

However, it appeared that the environmental credit from biogas and compost was not balanced with the environmental impact from the manure management processes owing to the high emissions from the biogas plant, waste water treatment and composting plant, although the alternative system led to considerable impact reduction of the farm. There were also uncertainties of the emissions during the storage stage of manure and effluent (Basset-Mens et al. 2007; Rigolot et al., 2010), for example the storage capacity and quality of effluent for application during cold season when the demand was low. It is still important to make efforts to control the disordered emissions during the management processes in the farm. Furthermore, only AP, EP and GWP as the most relevant impact factors were discussed in detail in this study. In par- ticular, toxicity and ecotoxicity impacts due to effluents and compost application were probably underestimated and there- fore just briefly considered here. A full inventory and estima- tion of impacts from these chemicals and their metabolites on plant growth, as well as their uptake by plants is desirable in further studies.

GWP for the current status, the current status + land application, and the alternative system. The mass flows in the pig stables and the impacts on the environment were the same for all described systems. The results indicated that the pig production contrib- uted most to AP and GWP, particularly in the alternative waste management system (84% of total AP and 93% of total GWP from pig production). In pig production, feed for pigs contrib- uted the most to GWP (61%) in this study. Besides feed (which is difficult for the farm to change), NH3 emission was a hotspot with high impact: 35% of total AP in pig production was due to the emissions from pig stables.

Biogas plant (combined with aeration plant). In the current system, the module biogas plant/waste water treatment plant con- tributed 64% to total EP compared to 41% if a part of the effluent was used for land application, and only 0.2% for the alternative system (Table 8). The significant improvement in EP was due to enhanced nitrogen and phosphorus recovery, as well as utilisation of effluent instead of discharging directly to the river. For AP, the reduction from the current to the alternative system was 22%; for GWP it was only 1%.
Composting plant. As shown in Table 8, the AP, EP and GWP from the composting process increased in the alternative scenario

as a result of the increased raw materials input, while all pig fae- ces would be used for composting. Considering the nutrient recy- cling from compost as credit, the environmental impacts per ton compost production would be reduced from the current to the alternative system. Waste air emissions during composting were the hotspots for AP, EP and GWP from the composting plant in this study.

Leaving pig production aside, the EP purely from the alter- native manure management system would be reduced by 91.8% compared with the 'current + land application' system, and 96.7% compared with the current system; the GWP would be reduced by 17.5% and 22%, respectively; and the AP would be reduced by 64.1% compared to the current system, but there would be no improvement from the 'current + land application system to the alternative system. The improvements between the 'current + land application' system and the alternative could be attributed to the new mass flow of all pig faeces for composting. The small disadvantage in AP due to the higher NH3 emission in composting plant would be outbalanced by the improvement of EP and GWP. It was possible to export part of the nutrients in compost from the pig farm with lower envi- ronmental impacts.

For the whole farm, there were improvements in total AP, EP and GWP by moving from the current manure management sys- tem to the alternative system. Nutrient recovery from effluents and faeces brought about a significant reduction in the environ- mental impacts of the farm. The alternative system was more environmental friendly in total. Feed was a hotspot for the main environmental impact categories of the farm, especially GWP, in this study. Similar results were reported by Eriksson et al. (2005) and Stone et al. (2011). However, there were other factors, such as manure mass flows, emissions and energy supply, that could be optimised by the farm to reduce the impact on the environ- ment. Similarly to the research by Prapaspongsa et al. (2010), the results of this study indicate that one of the effective ways for impact reduction in a pig farm is to enhance the nutrient and energy recovery at every stage, including in the control of NH3 and greenhouse gas emissions.

However, it appeared that the environmental credit from biogas and compost was not balanced with the environmental impact from the manure management processes owing to the high emissions from the biogas plant, waste water treatment and composting plant, although the alternative system led to considerable impact reduction of the farm. There were also uncertainties of the emissions during the storage stage of manure and effluent (Basset-Mens et al. 2007; Rigolot et al., 2010), for example the storage capacity and quality of effluent for application during cold season when the demand was low. It is still important to make efforts to control the disordered emissions during the management processes in the farm. Furthermore, only AP, EP and GWP as the most relevant impact factors were discussed in detail in this study. In par- ticular, toxicity and ecotoxicity impacts due to effluents and compost application were probably underestimated and there- fore just briefly considered here. A full inventory and estima- tion of impacts from these chemicals and their metabolites on plant growth, as well as their uptake by plants is desirable in further studies.

Nutrient recovery from effluents and faeces brought about a significant reduction in the environ- mental impacts of the farm. The alternative system was more environmental friendly in total. Feed was a hotspot for the main environmental impact categories of the farm, especially GWP, in this study. Similar results were reported by Eriksson et al. (2005) and Stone et al. (2011). However, there were other factors, such as manure mass flows, emissions and energy supply, that could be optimised by the farm to reduce the impact on the environ- ment. Similarly to the research by Prapaspongsa et al. (2010), the results of this study indicate that one of the effective ways for impact reduction in a pig farm is to enhance the nutrient and energy recovery at every stage, including in the control of NH3 and greenhouse gas emissions.

However, it appeared that the environmental credit from biogas and compost was not balanced with the environmental impact from the manure management processes owing to the high emissions from the biogas plant, waste water treatment and composting plant, although the alternative system led to considerable impact reduction of the farm. There were also uncertainties of the emissions during the storage stage of manure and effluent (Basset-Mens et al. 2007; Rigolot et al., 2010), for example the storage capacity and quality of effluent for application during cold season when the demand was low. It is still important to make efforts to control the disordered emissions during the management processes in the farm. Furthermore, only AP, EP and GWP as the most relevant impact factors were discussed in detail in this study. In par- ticular, toxicity and ecotoxicity impacts due to effluents and compost application were probably underestimated and there- fore just briefly considered here. A full inventory and estima- tion of impacts from these chemicals and their metabolites on plant growth, as well as their uptake by plants is desirable in further studies.
13: <header>Life cycle impact assessment </header>

All environmental impacts for the three manure management options are shown in Table 7. For the management system 'cur- rent + land application;, effluent partly used for land application led to considerable reduction of abiotic resource depletion poten- tial, AP, EP and GWP compared to the current system. All envi- ronmental impacts decreased from the current system to the alternative system, for example AP from 79.4 to 61.9 ton SO2-Eq, EP from 91.7 to 34.1 ton PO4-Eq, and GWP from 5714 to 5611 ton CO2-Eq. The AP, EP and GWP as the most relevant impact factors of each component of the farm are discussed in detail below.
Pig production. Table 8 shows the environment impact assess- ment results of each component (pig production, biogas/waste water treatment, composting) of the pig farm to total AP, EP and

GWP for the current status, the current status + land application, and the alternative system. The mass flows in the pig stables and the impacts on the environment were the same for all described systems. The results indicated that the pig production contrib- uted most to AP and GWP, particularly in the alternative waste management system (84% of total AP and 93% of total GWP from pig production). In pig production, feed for pigs contrib- uted the most to GWP (61%) in this study. Besides feed (which is difficult for the farm to change), NH3 emission was a hotspot with high impact: 35% of total AP in pig production was due to the emissions from pig stables.

Biogas plant (combined with aeration plant). In the current system, the module biogas plant/waste water treatment plant con- tributed 64% to total EP compared to 41% if a part of the effluent was used for land application, and only 0.2% for the alternative system (Table 8). The significant improvement in EP was due to enhanced nitrogen and phosphorus recovery, as well as utilisation of effluent instead of discharging directly to the river. For AP, the reduction from the current to the alternative system was 22%; for GWP it was only 1%.
Composting plant. As shown in Table 8, the AP, EP and GWP from the composting process increased in the alternative scenario

as a result of the increased raw materials input, while all pig fae- ces would be used for composting. Considering the nutrient recy- cling from compost as credit, the environmental impacts per ton compost production would be reduced from the current to the alternative system. Waste air emissions during composting were the hotspots for AP, EP and GWP from the composting plant in this study.

Leaving pig production aside, the EP purely from the alter- native manure management system would be reduced by 91.8% compared with the 'current + land application' system, and 96.7% compared with the current system; the GWP would be reduced by 17.5% and 22%, respectively; and the AP would be reduced by 64.1% compared to the current system, but there would be no improvement from the 'current + land application system to the alternative system. The improvements between the 'current + land application' system and the alternative could be attributed to the new mass flow of all pig faeces for composting. The small disadvantage in AP due to the higher NH3 emission in composting plant would be outbalanced by the improvement of EP and GWP. It was possible to export part of the nutrients in compost from the pig farm with lower envi- ronmental impacts.

For the whole farm, there were improvements in total AP, EP and GWP by moving from the current manure management sys- tem to the alternative system. Nutrient recovery from effluents and faeces brought about a significant reduction in the environ- mental impacts of the farm. The alternative system was more environmental friendly in total. Feed was a hotspot for the main environmental impact categories of the farm, especially GWP, in this study. Similar results were reported by Eriksson et al. (2005) and Stone et al. (2011). However, there were other factors, such as manure mass flows, emissions and energy supply, that could be optimised by the farm to reduce the impact on the environ- ment. Similarly to the research by Prapaspongsa et al. (2010), the results of this study indicate that one of the effective ways for impact reduction in a pig farm is to enhance the nutrient and energy recovery at every stage, including in the control of NH3 and greenhouse gas emissions.

However, it appeared that the environmental credit from biogas and compost was not balanced with the environmental impact from the manure management processes owing to the high emissions from the biogas plant, waste water treatment and composting plant, although the alternative system led to considerable impact reduction of the farm. There were also uncertainties of the emissions during the storage stage of manure and effluent (Basset-Mens et al. 2007; Rigolot et al., 2010), for example the storage capacity and quality of effluent for application during cold season when the demand was low. It is still important to make efforts to control the disordered emissions during the management processes in the farm. Furthermore, only AP, EP and GWP as the most relevant impact factors were discussed in detail in this study. In par- ticular, toxicity and ecotoxicity impacts due to effluents and compost application were probably underestimated and there- fore just briefly considered here. A full inventory and estima- tion of impacts from these chemicals and their metabolites on plant growth, as well as their uptake by plants is desirable in further studies.
14: On the basis of the results obtained, it is clear that the recycling of nutrients from effluent for land application and the enhanced export by compost cause less environmental threats in the alter- native scenario than the current status. The cropland area demand for sustainable land application of the effluent can be reduced by 42% if the faeces are completely used for composting. It is pos- sible to transfer 29% of total nitrogen, 87% of phosphorus, 34% of potassium and 75% of magnesium to the compost and reduce the total AP, EP and GWP of manure management compared with the current system on the farm. The alternative system is more environmental friendly in total if compared with the 'current + land application' system, in spite of small disadvantages in AP and the biogas yield. The advanced waste management option not only recovers and exports more nutrients from areas with high livestock density to arable land, but also decreases the main environmental impacts of the pig farm. The key for reduction of
environmental impacts on pig farms is an effective manure man- agement system that reduces emissions and enhances nutrient recovery.

As implied in this study, the cropland area demand for the pig farm is still high, even with an optimised manure management system and without regard to the toxicity of chemicals such as antibiotics. It is necessary to investigate the background data of the regional nutrients flow in order to develop sustainable live- stock systems under consideration of regional specific conditions.
